categories.title: How Aruvi plans your lessons
categories.description: What Aruvi is for; how it approaches lesson planning; how each subject stream works

categories.title: How Aruvi builds your assessments
categories.description: Purpose of assessment; how implied LOs drive question type and depth; teacher guide and inclusivity

categories.description: Annual period budgets, chapter periods, and period durations; how Aruvi suggests, adjusts, and tracks allocation

categories.description: Navigating My Classes and My Lessons; preparing lessons, documents, profile, notes, and help

categories.title: What Aruvi cannot do
categories.description: Skill boundary; what teachers often ask for that Aruvi does not produce

pairs.question: How can Aruvi help me as a teacher?
pairs.answer: Aruvi generates a complete lesson plan and assessment for any NCERT chapter across five subjects: Social Sciences (Grades VI–IX), Science (Grades VI–IX), Mathematics and English (Grades III–IX), and The World Around Us (Grades III–V). You enter your available periods — Aruvi builds a fully sequenced, NCF-aligned lesson plan and a matched chapter assessment. It reduces your planning time while ensuring every plan is traceable to the curriculum framework.

pairs.question: Can you explain what is meant by an NCF-aligned lesson plan?
pairs.answer: An NCF-aligned lesson plan is one whose elements are traceable to the National Curriculum Framework 2023. In Aruvi this rests on three things: activities are drawn from the NCF-compliant NCERT textbook — nothing is invented; they are structured using NCF's Pedagogy guidance for your subject and stage; and every assessment item is derived from the learning outcome implied by a competency-aligned textbook activity. Alongside the plan, Aruvi presents the chapter's competency mapping with justification from the textbook — evidence of how the chapter's content embodies the NCF competency framework. How activities are sequenced differs by subject, because textbook architecture differs — the alignment does not.

pairs.question: How can Aruvi help me as a curriculum planner or administrator?
pairs.answer: Aruvi's Annual Plan — part of My Lessons — gives you a whole-year view of teaching time across all chapters for a subject and class. It shows how the annual period budget should be distributed based on each chapter's demand, surfaces chapters that are heavier or lighter, and tracks your actual allocation against the suggestion as plans are prepared. This gives a curriculum planner a defensible, curriculum-grounded annual plan — not a guess.

pairs.question: How can Aruvi help if I am home-schooling my child or supporting self-study?
pairs.answer: Aruvi generates a structured path through any NCERT chapter — activities sequenced to build understanding progressively, with clear learning outcomes at each step. The paired assessment lets you check understanding at the end of each chapter. A home educator or self-directed student gets the same NCF-aligned plan a classroom teacher would use, scaled to their own pace and available time.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi ensure policy compliance with NCF 2023?
pairs.answer: Every Aruvi output is traceable to NCF 2023. Competency codes from the NCF framework are embedded in the chapter mapping. Activities are drawn from the NCF Pedagogy document for the relevant subject and stage. Lesson plans and assessments are generated within a set of subject-specific rules — versioned, internally maintained documents that translate NCF policy into precise generation constraints. No output is generated outside these rules.

pairs.question: What is the common approach across all subjects to lesson planning?
pairs.answer: Across all four subjects, the mechanism is the same: the chapter's own content drives the lesson plan, the lesson plan generates implied learning outcomes for each activity, and only those outcomes pass through to the assessment. The organising principle — how activities are sequenced within a chapter — differs by subject, because NCERT textbook architecture differs by subject. The pipeline is universal; the logic inside it is subject-specific.

pairs.question: What is the difference between a competency and a learning outcome in Aruvi?
pairs.answer: A competency is a durable cognitive ability defined in the NCF framework — for example, 'interpret historical sources.' It develops across many chapters over a stage. A learning outcome is chapter-specific and activity-specific: it names exactly what a student can do after completing one activity in this chapter. Aruvi calls these implied learning outcomes because they are derived from the activity, not imported from an external list.

pairs.question: What is an implied Learning Outcome? How is it used across subject streams?
pairs.answer: An implied Learning Outcome (implied LO) is a precise, observable statement of what a student can do after completing a specific lesson plan activity. It is called implied because Aruvi derives it from the activity itself — not from a published LO list. In all four subjects, implied LOs are the primary bridge between the lesson plan and the assessment: the assessment reads them to determine what each question must test.

pairs.question: Are lesson plans based on external learning outcomes? If not, why?
pairs.answer: No. Board-published or externally prescribed learning outcomes are not used as inputs. Learning outcomes in Aruvi are derived from what each chapter's activities actually demand of a student — they are implied by the activity, not imported from outside. Using external LOs would disconnect the assessment from what was actually taught. Aruvi's approach ensures the LO is a precise record of what happened in the lesson, not a generic curriculum statement.

pairs.question: Why does the NCERT textbook architecture differ by subject, and what is the implication for Aruvi's design?
pairs.answer: Social Sciences chapters sprawl across multiple competencies in named sections. Science chapters are built around a central cognitive operation — occasionally two, where the chapter genuinely integrates distinct operations. English is structured around six language skill spines applied to each text. Mathematics has an innate progression of worked examples, activities, and exercises. Because the textbook architectures are genuinely different, forcing one planning template across all subjects would flatten those differences. Aruvi uses four subject-specific sets of generation rules so each plan respects the textbook's own logic.

pairs.question: How does Social Sciences plan lessons?
pairs.answer: Social Sciences chapters develop several competencies across named sections. Aruvi walks the chapter section by section, anchoring each activity to a specific section of the textbook. Higher-weighted competencies receive more activities and more time. Each activity ends with one implied learning outcome — a single sentence describing what a student who completed that activity can now do. These implied LOs are the only input the assessment receives.

pairs.question: For Social Sciences, what are the three types of competencies and how do the lesson plan and assessment reflect the difference?
pairs.answer: Central competencies (weight 3) organise the chapter — their removal would dissolve its teaching logic. Substantive competencies (weight 2) have dedicated architectural presence across sections. Present competencies (weight 1) appear through at least one structural element. In the lesson plan, higher-weight competencies receive more activities and time. In the assessment, Central competencies receive an open task and extended questions; Substantive get short constructed responses; Present get diagnostic MCQs.

pairs.question: How does The World Around Us (TWAU) plan lessons?
pairs.answer: TWAU chapters are organised around named textbook sections. Aruvi walks these sections in reading order, anchoring one activity per period to each section's own content, tasks, and observations. Every period carries a dominant pedagogical mode drawn from the NCF's five prescribed approaches for the Preparatory Stage: Observe and Record, Hands-on Investigation, Discussion and Connection, Create and Express, or Reflect and Act. The chapter's two strands — a natural phenomenon and the human-cultural response to it — are developed together through this section sequence. Each period ends with one implied learning outcome stating the transferable skill the student has developed.

pairs.question: How does Science plan lessons at the Middle Stage (Grades VI–VIII)?
pairs.answer: Middle Stage Science chapters build one central cognitive operation through clear, ordered stages — from first encounter with a phenomenon to the highest-order thinking the chapter is designed to produce. Aruvi derives this cognitive progression from the chapter and plans activities along its stages. Each stage ends with one implied learning outcome. Activities are drawn exclusively from the chapter's own experiments, exercises, and exploratory prompts — nothing is invented.

pairs.question: How does Science plan lessons at the Secondary Stage (Grade IX)?
pairs.answer: Secondary Stage Science chapters are planned section by section, following the chapter's own section order — not a designed cognitive arc. Every activity is anchored to a named section of the chapter summary; content from outside that section is not introduced. Each section produces one implied learning outcome stating the transferable skill the student develops. Where the chapter integrates two distinct cognitive operations across sections, both are reflected in the lesson plan. Activities are drawn exclusively from the chapter's content — nothing is invented.

pairs.question: Why does Science use fewer Core Competencies per chapter than Social Sciences?
pairs.answer: Science chapters are built as self-contained competency units — each is designed to produce one primary cognitive ability. In rare cases where a chapter genuinely develops a second, cognitively independent operation across its own named sections, a co-central competency is recognised alongside the primary one. Social Sciences chapters, by contrast, routinely sprawl across multiple competencies simultaneously, with different sections developing different thinking skills. Because the textbook architectures are genuinely different, the generation rules differ by subject.

pairs.question: How does English plan lessons? What are the six spines?
pairs.answer: English lesson planning is built on six skill spines: Reading for Comprehension, Listening, Speaking, Writing, Vocabulary and Grammar, and Beyond-the-Text. For each main section of a chapter — poem, prose, dialogue — Aruvi walks all six spines and produces one implied learning outcome per (section × spine) pair. NCF competency codes sit underneath, but the teacher engages with spines, not codes. Each outcome directly drives a question in the assessment.

pairs.question: In English, how does the spine structure align with NCF?
pairs.answer: The six spines directly correspond to the language competency domains NCF 2023 defines for the language subject group. The teacher sees the spine structure — which is how language pedagogy is naturally organised — while the platform maintains full NCF alignment underneath. This keeps the plan usable for teachers without requiring them to navigate the framework directly.

pairs.question: How does Mathematics plan lessons?
pairs.answer: Mathematics chapters have an innate progression: worked examples introduce a method, activities make it tangible, exercises drill it. Each chapter also builds on the chapter before it. Aruvi respects this: each period is anchored to specific worked examples, activities, and exercise questions from the textbook, in order. Prior chapter knowledge is treated as the entry condition — the plan begins where the previous chapter left off, not at an abstract competency statement.

pairs.question: For Mathematics, why does Aruvi follow textbook flow more closely than other subjects?
pairs.answer: Mathematics is the only subject where the NCERT textbook itself already encodes a rigorous progression. The worked-example sequence, in-chapter activities, and exercise sets are not supplementary — they are the method. Aruvi co-opts the textbook's own architecture rather than overlaying an external structure. Departing from the textbook flow would introduce gaps in the method chain and undermine the coherence the textbook was designed to provide.

pairs.question: What is the role of stage-wise pedagogy in lesson plan development?
pairs.answer: The NCF Pedagogy document for each subject and stage specifies what kinds of activities are appropriate — inquiry-based approaches for Science, source analysis for Social Sciences, language task structures for English, and so on. Aruvi uses the pedagogy document to govern how an activity is structured. The chapter summary governs what the activity contains. The two work together: pedagogy determines the approach; chapter content determines the material.

pairs.question: By following the lesson plans, how do I achieve stage-wise Curricular Goals?
pairs.answer: Each chapter is mapped to NCF competencies, and each competency is linked to a Curricular Goal. A Curricular Goal is developed across many chapters over a stage — no single chapter achieves it. By using Aruvi plans consistently across the year, a teacher builds toward each Curricular Goal progressively.

pairs.question: Where do activities come from? Does Aruvi invent them?
pairs.answer: Activities are not invented. Every activity in a lesson plan is anchored to a named section of the chapter — it must be derivable from actual textbook content. The pedagogical approach — discussion, inquiry, map work, experiment, group task — is drawn from the NCF Pedagogy document for the relevant subject and stage. The chapter content and pedagogy document together produce the activity. Nothing generic or unanchored is introduced.

pairs.question: Why is there suggested homework in the lesson plan?
pairs.answer: Homework in Aruvi is not supplementary content — it is a deliberate extension of the period's cognitive work. Each homework suggestion is derived from the same chapter section and progression stage as the period's activity. It gives students time to consolidate or extend what the period introduced, without requiring additional classroom time. It is a suggestion, not a prescription — teachers adapt it to their school's homework norms.

pairs.question: Why is TWAU treated as a separate subject from Science and Social Sciences?
pairs.answer: At the Preparatory Stage, NCF 2023 does not separate natural and social world learning into distinct subjects. TWAU integrates both into a single inquiry-oriented subject. The textbook architecture is also different — chapters are built around a child's observable world rather than disciplinary concepts. Aruvi therefore uses TWAU-specific generation rules rather than applying Science or Social Sciences logic, which would misrepresent how these chapters are designed to be taught.

pairs.question: What competencies does TWAU develop, and how are they different from middle-stage subjects?
pairs.answer: TWAU develops observation, description, classification, and simple causal reasoning — foundational cognitive operations appropriate for Grades III–V. These are precursors to the subject-specific competencies that Science and Social Sciences develop at the Middle Stage. Unlike middle-stage subjects, TWAU competencies are not differentiated by discipline — a single chapter may develop observation of a natural phenomenon and awareness of a community practice within the same activity sequence.

pairs.question: Why does the lesson plan not include time for assessment?
pairs.answer: The lesson plan uses the full period budget for teaching. Assessment is a separate activity designed to follow the teaching sequence — it is generated alongside the lesson plan but is not embedded in it. This is a deliberate design principle: teaching time is fully protected, and assessment is not allowed to erode it. The teacher chooses when to administer the assessment after the chapter's teaching is complete.

pairs.question: What is a Learning Unit?
pairs.answer: A Learning Unit is one period's worth of teaching — the building block of every Aruvi lesson plan. The plan appears as a sequence of Learning Units on a progress rail showing what is done, what is current, and what lies ahead. Each unit carries its own overview, materials, teaching activities and homework, and — where applicable — assessment items. As you teach, you move the pointer forward, so Aruvi always knows where each section stopped.

pairs.question: Why does my lesson plan not show learning outcomes?
pairs.answer: This is deliberate. Implied learning outcomes are derived from each activity and are reserved for the assessment — they define exactly what each question must test. The lesson plan shows what you and your students actually do: activities, materials, and homework. Displaying outcomes inside the plan would add framework language without helping you run the period. You will meet each outcome in the assessment, attached to the question that tests it.

pairs.question: What is the Annual Plan in My Lessons?
pairs.answer: The Annual Plan shows how your annual period budget for a subject and class spreads across its chapters. Aruvi suggests a distribution based on each chapter's weight or effort index, and as you prepare lessons it tracks your actual allocation against that suggestion on an ongoing basis. Use it to see whether the year is running ahead or behind, and to decide how many periods the remaining chapters can receive.

pairs.question: What do we mean by Assessment in Aruvi? What is the purpose?
pairs.answer: Assessment in Aruvi is a chapter-scoped, formative tool — generated alongside the lesson plan and designed to test whether students engaged with the chapter's activities in the spirit of developing its competencies. It is not an exam. Every question is traceable to an implied learning outcome from the lesson plan. Its purpose is to help the teacher see where understanding has formed and where it has not, at the chapter level.

pairs.question: How is Aruvi assessment different from exams administered periodically to students?
pairs.answer: Periodic exams are summative — they test retention across many chapters at a fixed point. Aruvi assessments are formative and chapter-scoped — administered after teaching a single chapter or single unit, designed to reveal the quality of student engagement with that chapter's specific activities. They do not carry marks or contribute to certification. The teacher guide that accompanies each question is designed for diagnostic use, not grading.

pairs.question: Are Aruvi assessments 'for', 'of', or 'as' learning?
pairs.answer: Aruvi assessments are primarily assessment for learning — they give the teacher diagnostic information to inform what comes next. They are not assessment of learning (certification or ranking) and not assessment as learning (student self-evaluation). Every question includes a teacher guide that names the learning outcome being tested and — for MCQs — explains what each wrong answer reveals about the student's specific misunderstanding.

pairs.question: Why does the assessment test Learning Outcomes and not the competency directly?
pairs.answer: A competency develops across many chapters and cannot be fully assessed at the chapter level. An implied learning outcome is chapter-specific and activity-specific — it names exactly what a student should be able to do after one lesson plan activity. Testing the implied LO tests whether the specific teaching in this chapter produced its intended cognitive result. This keeps assessment honest and prevents generic competency questions that float free of what was actually taught.

pairs.question: Does the assessment cover the whole chapter?
pairs.answer: Yes. Since the Learning Units of the lesson plan are organised to cover the entire chapter, the assessment — which is derived from the lesson plan's implied LOs — itself covers the entire chapter. Assessment length is not capped: chapters with more competencies produce longer assessments.

pairs.question: Why does the number of questions differ across chapters?
pairs.answer: Assessment length in Aruvi is intentionally uncapped — it reflects how much the chapter itself demands. A chapter with more implied learning outcomes produces more questions. In Social Sciences, chapters with more or higher-weight competencies generate more LOs. In Science, more sections (or progression stages at the Middle Stage) produce more implied LOs and therefore more questions. In English, more active spines per section add more outcomes. In Mathematics, more worked-example steps add more outcome pairs. Length is a function of chapter content, not an arbitrary target.

pairs.question: Where do I find the assessment?
pairs.answer: Inside the lesson plan itself. Each question is anchored to the Learning Unit whose teaching it tests — open a unit's Assess tab to see its items alongside what was taught. Units that anchor no question have no Assess tab. For a single consolidated document, generate the assessment as a PDF or Word file from My Lessons — together with the lesson plan or separately, and with or without suggested answers.

pairs.question: What do the four tabs on an assessment item show?
pairs.answer: Each assessment item has four tabs. Overview states the learning outcome being tested, with the question type, cognitive demand, and competency where recorded. Question is the class-facing view — stem, stimulus, and plain options — safe to project or read out. Answer holds the correct option or model answer with the diagnostic guidance, and appears only when populated. Inclusivity carries the scaffolding and extension suggestions. Students should see only the Question tab.

pairs.question: Why are assessments linked to units and not summarised at the end of the chapter?
pairs.answer: Because every question tests the implied learning outcome of a specific activity, Aruvi shows it beside the Learning Unit that produced that outcome. You always see what a question tests and where it was taught — making review and re-teaching precise. Administration remains your choice: conduct a unit's questions when the unit ends, or complete the chapter and administer them together as one assessment.

pairs.question: Why are there sometimes multiple assessment items under a unit and no assessments for some units?
pairs.answer: Question count follows the chapter's own structure, not an even spread. A unit that produces several implied learning outcomes — or one whose outcome demands both a diagnostic MCQ and a deeper task — anchors several items. A unit whose work is consolidated elsewhere may anchor none; its learning is tested through a question on a related unit. Coverage stays complete at chapter level: every mapped competency is assessed.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in Social Sciences?
pairs.answer: In Social Sciences, competency weight governs question type and count. Central competencies (weight 3) receive MCQs, short constructed responses, and exactly one open task per assessment. Substantive competencies (weight 2) receive MCQs and short constructed responses. Present competencies (weight 1) receive MCQs only. Every question cites the implied LO it tests by period number. Weight numbers are never shown to students — only Central, Substantive, Present labels are used.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in Middle Stage Science (Grades VI–VIII)?
pairs.answer: In Middle Stage Science, question type follows the chapter's cognitive progression stages. Foundational stages receive MCQs. Middle stages add short constructed responses. The final stage — the highest-order cognitive operation — receives the open task. Every question is traceable to the implied LO of its progression stage. MCQs at every stage include diagnostically intentional distractors with a teacher guide explaining what each wrong answer reveals about the student's specific misunderstanding.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in Secondary Stage Science (Grade IX)?
pairs.answer: In Secondary Stage Science, question format is governed by the cognitive demand of each section — not by its position in the chapter. Recall and Understanding sections receive MCQs with diagnostic distractors. Application sections receive short constructed responses where the method chain is the object of assessment. Analysis and Evaluation sections receive extended constructed responses. Where a chapter has a co-central section integrating two distinct operations, that section receives an open task. Every question is traceable to the implied LO of its anchored section.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in English?
pairs.answer: In English, each implied learning outcome corresponds to a (section × spine) pair from the lesson plan. The assessment generates one question per implied LO. A Reading outcome gets a comprehension question on the actual passage. A Writing outcome gets a writing task tied to the same passage. A Speaking or Listening outcome gets a structured task the teacher can facilitate in class. Every question is grounded in the chapter's actual text — nothing is generic.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in Mathematics?
pairs.answer: In Mathematics, each implied learning outcome pairs with two artefacts in the assessment: a freshly written question that tests whether the method generalises to an unfamiliar context, and a companion exercise drawn from the textbook's own exercise set. This tests both transferable understanding and internalisation of the textbook's own progression. The teacher sees both side by side for each outcome.

pairs.question: Within the approach to Maths assessment, is there a difference between the Middle Stage and the Preparatory Stage?
pairs.answer: Yes. In the Middle Stage, each period produces one implied learning outcome, and the assessment generates one item per period. In the Preparatory Stage, the unit of work is finer — implied outcomes are generated at the individual task level (a worked example, an in-chapter activity, or an exercise set), so a single period may produce multiple outcomes and multiple assessment items. This reflects the nature of early Mathematics learning: at Grades III–V, each discrete task introduces or drills a distinct micro-skill, and collapsing several tasks into one period-level outcome would obscure whether a student has actually grasped each step in the method chain.

pairs.question: How is the assessment rubric developed in TWAU?
pairs.answer: TWAU has no competency weights — all mapped competencies sit at equal depth. The assessment generates exactly one item per period. The period's dominant pedagogical mode determines the question type — ranging from MCQs for observational periods through to open tasks for creative and reflective ones. When a Reflect-and-Act period targets a physical behaviour, the open task becomes a performance task with an observation rubric. Where answers depend on regional context, a teacher note confirms that locally varied answers are acceptable.

pairs.question: How are question types chosen in the assessment?
pairs.answer: Question type is prescribed by Aruvi's generation rules for each subject — it is not chosen freely by the AI. In Social Sciences, competency weight determines type. In Science, progression stage determines type at the Middle Stage; cognitive demand of each section determines type at the Secondary Stage. In English, each spine outcome maps to a specific question form. In Mathematics, every outcome gets a transfer question and a textbook exercise. The permitted types are MCQ, short constructed response, extended constructed response, and open task.

pairs.question: What are MCQ distractors and how can they help a teacher?
pairs.answer: In Aruvi assessments, every wrong option in an MCQ is written with diagnostic intent — it represents a specific, known misconception or reasoning error students typically make. The teacher guide for each MCQ explains exactly what a student who chose each wrong option most likely misunderstood. This turns a multiple-choice question into a diagnostic instrument: the teacher does not just see how many got it wrong, but why.

pairs.question: Do assessments have answers, and how are they different for closed questions (like MCQs) and open-ended ones?
pairs.answer: Yes. Every question carries answer support for the teacher, never shown to students. For closed questions like MCQs, the correct option is marked and each wrong option explains the misunderstanding it reveals. For constructed responses, expected answer elements or look-for markers describe what a good answer contains. For open tasks, the guide describes what a strong response demonstrates. Assessment documents can be generated with or without these suggested answers.

pairs.question: What is inclusivity and its purpose in assessment?
pairs.answer: Most assessments include an inclusivity note — a practical suggestion on two fronts: how to scaffold the question for students who are struggling, and how to extend the cognitive demand for students who need a higher challenge. This is embedded in the guide alongside the question, not in a separate worksheet. It gives the teacher a differentiation cue at the point of use, without requiring separate preparation.

pairs.question: Why do we not have a marking scheme for the assessment? How can I work around it?
pairs.answer: Aruvi does not generate traditional marking schemes with point allocations because the assessments are formative, not certification instruments. Instead, every question has a teacher guide: for MCQs, it names what each wrong answer indicates; for constructed responses, it lists expected elements or look-for markers; for open tasks, it describes strong-versus-weak response characteristics. If your school requires a numerical scheme, you can construct one from these guide elements.

pairs.question: What is an open task and when does it appear?
pairs.answer: An open task is a constructed-response question requiring the student to demonstrate a competency in an extended, integrated way — a poster, letter, diagram, explanation, or role play. There is exactly one open task per assessment. In Social Sciences, it is assigned to the Central competency with the most implied LOs. In Science, it appears at the final, highest-order progression stage. The teacher guide describes what a strong response demonstrates.

pairs.question: Can one question test more than one Learning Outcome?
pairs.answer: Yes. In Social Sciences assessments, a question may synthesise across several implied LOs from related activities — provided the guide block names each LO being tested by period number. This reflects the reality that some competencies are developed cumulatively across a chapter. The question tests the student's ability to reason across what several activities built, not just the output of one.

pairs.question: Should I conduct assessments alongside chapter teaching, at the end of each chapter, or at the end of term?
pairs.answer: Aruvi assessments are designed to follow the completion of a chapter's teaching sequence — not at term end. They are chapter-scoped: every question assumes the student has completed all the activities of the unit(s) to which the assessment is linked. So they can be conducted at the end of the unit or chapter. Administering at term end would mix chapter content and reduce diagnostic value.

pairs.question: What are the three aspects relating to time when it comes to teaching in a school?
pairs.answer: Three time decisions shape teaching. First, the annual teaching time for a subject in a class — the total periods available for the year. Second, the periods available to teach a particular chapter within that subject. Third, the duration of a period — and a school may run more than one duration type, for example a 40-minute regular period and a 60-minute double or laboratory period. Aruvi works with all three.

pairs.question: Does Aruvi allow me to set all these three as per my requirements?
pairs.answer: Yes. You set the annual period budget for each subject and class, the periods for any chapter, and one or more period durations — all remain adjustable. To guide you, Aruvi's own algorithm proposes how a given annual teaching time should be spread across the various chapters, based on each chapter's weight or effort index. The proposal is a curriculum-grounded starting point; every number stays yours to change.

pairs.question: What is the importance of defining an annual period budget for a particular subject and a class?
pairs.answer: The annual budget anchors everything downstream. Once Aruvi knows the total periods available for a subject and class, it can distribute them across chapters in proportion to each chapter's demand — so no chapter silently consumes time another chapter needs. It also lets you monitor the year as you teach: how much time is committed, how much remains, and whether the remaining chapters fit the remaining periods.

pairs.question: Does NCF suggest annual periods to be allocated to various subjects?
pairs.answer: Yes. NCF 2023 recommends annual teaching periods for each subject, defined stage-wise — Preparatory, Middle, and Secondary. They are advisory in nature, not prescriptive: each school should consider its local context — calendar, timetable, and priorities — and set its annual budget appropriately. Aruvi accepts whatever figure you decide.

pairs.question: Where do I set the annual periods for a subject for a given class in Aruvi?
pairs.answer: In your class profile. When you set up — or later edit — a subject and class under Settings, Aruvi asks for the annual period budget. If you do not know the number, the estimator helps you arrive at it from teaching weeks, working days, or a direct estimate. The budget you set there drives the Annual Plan's chapter-wise distribution.

pairs.question: Where do I set the teaching time for a chapter?
pairs.answer: At the point of preparing the lesson: when you generate a chapter's plan, you enter the periods you plan to allocate for it, and the plan is built exactly to that budget. For guidance, the Annual Plan tab lets you look at Aruvi's suggested distribution of the annual available time across the various chapters — and then monitor your actual allocation against it on an ongoing basis as you prepare lessons.

pairs.question: How do we set the duration of periods? Can I set more than one type of duration?
pairs.answer: Period durations are part of your class profile. During setup — or any time from Settings — you define the duration types your school runs for a subject and class. More than one type is fully supported: for example, a 40-minute standard period alongside a 60-minute double or laboratory period. When you prepare a lesson, you tell Aruvi how many periods of each duration you have, and it plans to that mix.

pairs.question: I have periods of different duration within a week. How does Aruvi reckon this in suggesting a lesson plan?
pairs.answer: Aruvi plans each Learning Unit for a specific period duration — activities and their phases are sized in minutes to fit the period they belong to. When your week mixes durations, you enter how many periods of each type the chapter has; longer periods receive proportionately more work. The plan therefore reflects your real timetable rather than assuming every period is the same length.

pairs.question: Will I be able to complete an activity within the time indicated in the phases in minutes?
pairs.answer: The phase timings are made as realistic as possible — they are derived from what the activity actually demands, not spread evenly across the period. Real classrooms still vary, and that is expected. The Learning Unit model absorbs this: if an activity runs over, carry it forward and begin the next period where you stopped — the pointer keeps your place. Treat the minutes as a well-grounded guide, not a stopwatch.

pairs.question: Why do some chapters get more periods than others?
pairs.answer: Period allocation in Aruvi reflects how much classroom effort a chapter genuinely requires — not how long it is in pages. The measure of effort differs by subject: Social Sciences uses competency weight; Science, English, and Mathematics use an effort index built from signals specific to each subject's architecture. The result is a defensible default allocation the teacher or administrator can audit and override for any chapter.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi suggest periods for various chapters for Social Sciences?
pairs.answer: Each chapter's weight is the sum of its competency weight scores: Central competencies contribute 3, Substantive contribute 2, Present contribute 1. Aruvi allocates teaching periods in proportion to chapter weight across the full year. A chapter developing three Central competencies receives substantially more periods than one developing a single Present competency.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi suggest periods for various chapters for Science?
pairs.answer: Science chapters are allocated periods using an effort index — a composite score built from four signals: conceptual demand of the exercises, student-executed activity load, teacher demonstration load, and exercise execution load (whether students must produce diagrams or multi-step calculations). Activity count and demo count are first converted to discrete bounded tiers before they are combined — this prevents a single high-count chapter from distorting the index. These factors are combined, with appropriate weights, into a single effort index. Higher effort index chapters receive more periods. The breakdown is visible in the Annual Plan — the score is never opaque.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi suggest periods for various chapters for English?
pairs.answer: English chapters are allocated periods using an effort index built from four spine-based signals: spine load (how many of the six spines the chapter actively develops), task density (how many graded tasks the chapter sets), writing demand (the weight of long-form writing required), and project load (the scale of any Beyond-the-Text projects). These combine into an effort index that drives proportional allocation across the year.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi suggest periods for various chapters for Mathematics?
pairs.answer: Mathematics chapters are allocated periods using an effort index built from the same four signals as Science: conceptual demand of the exercises, student-executed activity load, teacher demonstration load, and exercise execution load. As in Science, activity count and demo count are first converted to discrete bounded tiers before the factors are combined, with appropriate weights, into a single effort index. Chapters with higher conceptual demand and denser activity sets receive more periods. The allocation reflects how much the textbook itself signals a chapter requires.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi suggest periods for various chapters for TWAU?
pairs.answer: TWAU chapters are allocated periods using an effort index built from signals appropriate to the Preparatory Stage: the number of observation or field tasks the chapter requires, the count of hands-on classroom activities, the reading and description load, and any project or craft element. Chapters with more student-executed observation tasks and higher description demand receive more periods. The allocation is visible in the Annual Plan and can be adjusted.

pairs.question: What is competency weight used in social sciences and how is it calculated?
pairs.answer: Competency weight is a numerical signal — 3, 2, or 1 — assigned to each competency mapped to a chapter. Weight 3 (Central): the chapter's teaching logic dissolves without this competency. Weight 2 (Substantive): the competency has dedicated architectural presence across multiple sections. Weight 1 (Present): it appears through at least one structural element. Weight is determined by applying structural tests to the chapter's architecture — not by content importance or difficulty.

pairs.question: What is the effort index?
pairs.answer: The effort index is Aruvi's measure of how much classroom effort a chapter requires — used for Science, English, and Mathematics. For Science and Mathematics, it is a weighted sum of four discrete-tier signals: conceptual demand , activity load, demo load , and exec load. Raw activity and demo counts are first bucketed into bounded tiers so no single high-count chapter can distort the index. The index is computed once during the mapping step and stored with the chapter record. It is the basis for Aruvi's suggested period allocation for those subjects.

pairs.question: Why does assessment not have time allocated to it within the chapter period budget?
pairs.answer: The period budget you enter is used fully for teaching — the assessment is generated alongside the plan but never eats into it. If you intend to administer the formative assessment in class, reduce that time before inputting the periods available for teaching the chapter. Note also that NCF's recommended annual teaching time excludes time allocated for summative examinations, so your annual budget should represent teaching time alone.

pairs.question: What happens if I allocate far more or far less time than suggested by Aruvi to a chapter?
pairs.answer: The plan is rebuilt honestly for whatever you enter — Aruvi rescales; it never pads or truncates arbitrarily. With far fewer periods, activities compress and each period must carry more, at some cost to depth of engagement. With far more, activities stretch, deepen, and consolidate. The suggestion reflects the chapter's measured demand, so large departures deserve a deliberate reason — and the Annual Plan shows what your choice does to the rest of the year.

pairs.question: What are the two main tabs in Aruvi and what does each do?
pairs.answer: Aruvi has two main tabs. My Classes is home — a card for each section you teach, showing its current chapter and how far you have progressed; tap a card to continue teaching. My Lessons is the repository — every lesson plan you have prepared, with its assessment and the Annual Plan view. '+ Prepare Lesson' is available in both tabs whenever you want to plan a new chapter. Your subjects, classes, and durations sit behind the Settings gear.

pairs.question: What happens the first time I use Aruvi?
pairs.answer: Aruvi opens with one task, not a set-up form. You choose a subject, a class, and a chapter; accept or adjust the suggested periods; and generate your first lesson plan. You then attach it to the section or sections you teach, and your workspace opens with a card for each. There is no upfront profile-building — Aruvi quietly records what it learns as you work, and you can complete your profile whenever you choose.

pairs.question: How do I generate and download PDF or Word documents of my lesson plan and assessment?
pairs.answer: Click the My Lessons tab and choose the document image on bottom right of the lesson plan card. You can generate the lesson plan and the assessment together in one document or as separate documents, in PDF or Word format. For the assessment, you choose whether to include the suggested answers and teacher guidance, or produce a clean version without them for use with students. A fresh document can be generated at any time from the saved plan.

pairs.question: How does Aruvi remember where I stopped my teaching with a particular section?
pairs.answer: Every section card carries its own lesson pointer. As you finish a Learning Unit, you move the pointer forward, and Aruvi records it for that section independently — 6A and 6B can follow the same lesson plan and still stand at different units. When you return, each card shows exactly where that section is, and opening it takes you straight to the current unit. No dates or deadlines are involved — only where you stopped.

pairs.question: How do I add another class, section, or subject?
pairs.answer: Two ways. The quickest is the big '+' button in My Classes — it opens a Subject · Class · Section chooser and walks you through the same short steps as your first lesson. Alternatively, open Settings and edit your class profile directly, where you can add subjects, classes, and sections in one place. Either route updates your profile, and new section cards appear in My Classes once a lesson is attached.

pairs.question: How do I set up my class profile in Aruvi?
pairs.answer: Your class profile holds what you teach: subjects, classes, sections, period durations, and the annual period budget for each subject-class. You never have to fill it upfront — it builds itself as you use Aruvi, with each lesson you prepare quietly adding its subject, class, and section. To build it proactively, open Settings and complete it directly: add subjects and classes, set durations, and enter annual budgets with the estimator's help.

pairs.question: Where are my saved plans stored?
pairs.answer: Saved plans live in your Aruvi account and appear in My Lessons, organised by subject and class. Each entry holds the lesson plan and, once generated, its assessment — linked under the same chapter record with a timestamp. Your plans are private to your account: sign in from any device and they are all there. No other teacher can see or change them.

pairs.question: Can I use Aruvi on my phone or tablet?
pairs.answer: Yes — Aruvi is designed mobile-first. Every screen is built to work comfortably on a phone held vertically, one-handed, during the school day: checking your classes, moving the lesson pointer, reading a Learning Unit before walking in. Tablets and desktops present the same experience with more room, which some teachers prefer for reading full plans or generating documents. Nothing requires a computer.

pairs.question: How do I archive lesson plans?
pairs.answer: From My Lessons: open the plan's options and choose Archive. Archiving removes the plan from your active list without deleting it — the plan, its assessment, and any notes remain stored and can be restored at any time. Use it at year end to clear completed chapters while keeping the record for next year. Archived plans stay private to your account, like everything else.

pairs.question: What are Chapter Notes and Period Notes? Where can I access them?
pairs.answer: Both are private jottings for your future self. Period Notes belong to one period of one section's plan — written from the period view after class, capturing what happened: time used, observations, what to carry forward. A small indicator shows where a note exists. Chapter Notes belong to the lesson plan itself — broader reflections recorded from the chapter view, typically at chapter end. Notes stay with their plan and accumulate over the years.

pairs.question: Is there a guided tour that explains the basic features of Aruvi?
pairs.answer: Yes. When your first lesson is ready, a short guided tour points out the essentials on the live screen: your section cards, the lesson pointer, the Learning Unit tabs, and the '+ Prepare Lesson' action. It takes under a minute and can be skipped at any point. For anything the tour does not cover, Ask Aruvi — this helpline — is available throughout the app.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi tell me which student has mastered which competency?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi generates lesson plans, assessments, and teacher guides — it does not process student responses or maintain individual student records. The assessment output tells you what each question tests and what each wrong answer reveals, but recording student performance, tracking mastery, or generating progress reports is outside Aruvi's scope. Aruvi is a planning and generation tool, not a student information system.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate marking schemes?
pairs.answer: Aruvi does not generate traditional marking schemes with point allocations. Instead, every question comes with a teacher guide: for MCQs, it names what each wrong answer indicates; for constructed responses, it lists expected answer elements or look-for markers; for open tasks, it describes strong-versus-weak response characteristics. If your school requires a numerical scheme, you would construct it from the guide content.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi track individual student performance?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi does not track, record, or profile individual students. It generates teaching and assessment tools for the teacher — it does not manage student data of any kind. Individual student tracking, progress records, and performance databases are outside Aruvi's scope entirely.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi prepare students for Board exams?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi generates chapter-scoped formative assessments aligned to NCF 2023 competencies. Board examination preparation — which involves summative testing, past paper practice, and exam-style question formats — is a different function. Aruvi is not designed for it and does not produce Board exam preparation materials.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate a summative test or term-end exam?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi generates formative assessments only — chapter-scoped, designed for a single session, and intended as a professional teaching tool rather than a certification instrument. Summative tests, term-end exams, half-yearly and annual examinations are outside Aruvi's scope.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi suggest how to adapt an activity for a large class?
pairs.answer: Ask Aruvi is scoped to questions about how the platform works. Questions about adapting activities for specific classroom conditions — class size, available space, mixed-ability groups — are beyond this scope. Your question will be automatically logged and forwarded to Aruvi management for follow-up.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate lesson plans for subjects not in NCERT — for example, a school's own curriculum?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi's pipeline is built entirely on NCERT textbook content and NCF 2023 competency mapping. It requires an NCERT chapter as its content input. Non-NCERT curricula — school-designed courses, alternative textbooks, or international curricula — are outside Aruvi's scope.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate a Holistic Progress Card input?
pairs.answer: No. Holistic Progress Cards are multi-dimensional, cross-subject instruments designed for whole-child reporting — they require inputs that go well beyond what a chapter-scoped assessment can provide. HPC generation is explicitly outside Aruvi's scope.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi suggest visual aids, worksheets, or physical materials for a lesson?
pairs.answer: Aruvi's lesson plan may note when a visual reference is helpful for a particular activity. However, Aruvi does not generate visual content, design worksheets, or specify physical materials. These are teacher professional decisions. The lesson plan describes the activity and its pedagogical intent — the teacher decides how to resource it.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi tell me whether my school is NCF 2023 compliant?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi generates NCF-aligned lesson plans and assessments — it is not a compliance auditing tool. Whether a school is NCF 2023 compliant depends on many factors beyond lesson plan generation. Aruvi management can discuss how the platform contributes to alignment, but compliance determination is outside the platform's scope.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate multi-session projects or cross-subject tasks?
pairs.answer: No. Every assessment task Aruvi generates must be completable in a single session with no external coordination. Multi-session projects, cross-subject integrated tasks, and activities requiring materials or preparation outside the classroom are explicitly outside Aruvi's assessment scope. This constraint ensures the assessment is executable by every teacher, in every school, regardless of resource availability.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi generate content in regional languages?
pairs.answer: Aruvi's generated lesson plans and assessments are currently in English. Ask Aruvi — this help feature — answers from Aruvi's curated knowledge base; it is available in English today, with additional Indian languages planned as the knowledge base is translated. Generation of lesson plans and assessments in regional-language mediums is not currently available.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi tell me what activities to use for a specific pedagogical goal I have in mind?
pairs.answer: Ask Aruvi is scoped to questions about how the platform works. Aruvi produces activities derived from chapter content and NCF pedagogy — the platform does not offer a reverse lookup from pedagogical goal to activity. Your question will be automatically logged and forwarded to Aruvi management for follow-up.

pairs.question: Why does Aruvi not cover the Foundational Stage?
pairs.answer: The Foundational Stage is built on play-based, experiential learning — the child learns through free exploration, storytelling, and guided play rather than structured classroom activities. Aruvi generates lesson plans and assessments anchored to textbook sections and period schedules, which is a classroom-based pedagogical model. In our view, imposing this structure on the Foundational Stage would work against the spirit of how young children learn best. We have therefore chosen not to extend the platform to Foundational Stage.

pairs.question: Why does Aruvi not cover Class X now?
pairs.answer: NCF 2023-compliant NCERT textbooks for Class X have not yet been released. Aruvi's pipeline is built entirely on such textbooks — every plan is anchored to their chapters and competency architecture — so the platform extends only to grades where this material exists. As and when the Class X textbooks are released, Aruvi will extend its coverage accordingly.

pairs.question: Does Aruvi send reminders or notifications?
pairs.answer: No — by design. Aruvi never asks 'what is due?'; it answers 'where did I stop?'. There are no deadlines, no overdue flags, and no notifications pushing you along. Your timetable is cyclic and your pace is your own: each section's pointer simply holds your place until you return. Teaching runs on the teacher's judgement, not on the tool's calendar.

pairs.question: Can Aruvi hold my school timetable?
pairs.answer: No. Aruvi deliberately keeps no timetable, calendar, or day grid. The only time facts it stores are numbers: period durations, periods per week, and the annual budget for each subject-class. That is enough to plan lessons and track progress without duplicating your school's timetable, which lives — and changes — at school. Where you are in a chapter is tracked by the pointer, not by dates.

pairs.question: Can anyone else see my plans and notes?
pairs.answer: No. Your plans, progress, and notes are private to your account — no other teacher and no administrator can view them. In turn, please be privacy-conscious in what you write: avoid using students' names or other identifying or confidential details in Chapter Notes, Period Notes, or anywhere else on the platform. Refer to situations rather than individuals, so your records never carry information with privacy implications.
